turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-1095, Thompson versus United States. Patrick Thompson took out three loans totaling $219,000 from the same bank. The bank later failed and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation or FDIC became responsible for collecting the outstanding loans. The FDIC sent Thompson a bill for $269,000 made up of the original $219,000, plus interest. Thompson called the FDIC. During that phone call, Thompson said that he had borrowed $110,000 from the bank. The government charged Thompson with violating 18 USC Section 1014, which criminalizes making any false statement to the FDIC. According to the government, Thompson falsely stated that he borrowed $110,000 when he had actually borrowed $219,000, but Thompson responded that his statement was actually true because he did take out a loan for $110,000 just as he said. He simply neglected to mention that he had also taken out two other loans. The Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals held that it did not need to consider that argument because it read Section 1014’s ban on false statement to cover misleading statements as well and Thompson’s statement were at least misleading. We granted review to determine whether Section 1014 criminalizes statements that are misleading but not false. We hold today that it does not. Section 1014 criminalizes "any false statement." False and misleading are two different things, because not every misleading statement is a false one. Say a doctor tells a perspective patient, I’ve done a hundred of these surgeries when 99 of those patients died on the table. The statement is not false but it is misleading because it would lead most people to think the surgeries were successful. Given that not every misleading statement is false, it is important that Section 1014 uses only the word false. After all, many other statutes in the United States Criminal Code use the phrase false or misleading showing that when Congress wanted to include both these types of statements, it knew how to do so. We therefore conclude that regardless of whether a statement is misleading, the proper question according to the text of the statute is whether the statement is false. The judgement of the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The opinion of the Court is unanimous. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion and Justice Jackson has also filed a concurring opinion.
